Off Calendar Apr-03-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances via Zoom:
Rabiah Oral, Esq. of Maune Raichle, appearing for the Plaintiff(s);
Rhonda Woo, Esq. of Nelson Mullins, appearing for Defendant Solar Turbines Inc.

Hearing re: DEFENDANT SOLAR TURBINES INCORPORATED MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Motion For Summary Adjudication

The matter is contested and argument is held.

Upon conclusion of argument, the Court takes the matter under submission.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman. Clerk: Johnny Sengmany. Reporter: Sheila Pham CSR #13293 T: 415.274.9977 E: calendar-la@veritext.com (304/EPS).

***After the hearing the court issues the following:

Defendant Solar Turbines Incorporated's ("Defendant") Motion for Summary Judgment or, in the Alternative Motion for Summary Adjudication, is DENIED in part and GRANTED in part. Opposition filed. Reply filed.

Plaintiff's objections to the Declaration of Kevin Klein at 3-4 are SUSTAINED on the grounds of inadmissible hearsay and lack of foundation.

Defendant's objections to Exhibit B, the Declaration of Robert P. Shultz at 2, 4-7 are OVERRULED. Defendant argues that Mr. Shultz's declaration is self-serving and is contradicted by his extensive deposition testimony. The Court disagrees. Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden of showing Plaintiff does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain needed evidence of asbestos exposure and causation. (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855.) Defendant argues that Plaintiff lacks evidence to establish that he was exposed to asbestos from a product for which Defendant was liable. The Court disagrees. For the reasons set forth below, the Court finds that Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden. Further, Plaintiff provides sufficient evidence in opposition to establish triable issues of material fact with respect to both causation and punitive damages.
2. Negligent Misrepresentation
Defendant's unopposed Motion for Summary Adjudication of Plaintiff's third cause of action for negligent misrepresentation is GRANTED. (Plaintiff's Response to SSUMF II:1.)
3. Fraud by Nondisclosure
Defendant's unopposed Motion for Summary Adjudication of Plaintiff's fourth cause of action for fraud by nondisclosure is GRANTED. (Plaintiff's Response to SSUMF III:1.)
4. Punitive Damages
Defendant's motion fails to show that Plaintiff is unable to provide facts that Defendant acted with malice, fraud, or oppression.

Hearing re: DEFENDANT PROGRESS RAIL LOCOMOTIVE INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Motion For Summary Adjudication

The matter is contested and argument is held.

Upon conclusion of argument, the Court takes the matter under submission.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman. Clerk: Johnny Sengmany. Reporter: Sheila Pham CSR #13293 T: 415.274.9977 E: calendar-la@veritext.com (304/EPS).

***After the hearing the court issues the following:

Defendant Progress Rail Locomotive, Inc.'s Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Defendant's Alternative Motion for Summary Adjudication is GRANTED in part and DENIED in part. Opposition filed. Reply filed.
A. Evidentiary Objections
a. Plaintiff's Objections
Declaration of Kevin Klein at 4-6, are SUSTAINED on grounds of lack of foundation and hearsay as to events and conditions prior to 1990. None of the facts Mr. Klein attests to occurred within the time period of his personal knowledge.
Exhibit 1 to the Declaration of Kevin Klein is SUSTAINED on the grounds of unauthenticated hearsay. (Evid. Code, 1200, et seq.)
b. Defendant's objections
Defendant's objections in reply are OVERRULED.
B. Motion for Summary Judgment
Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden of showing Plaintiff Alfonso Pagano Jr. ("Plaintiff") does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain needed evidence of asbestos exposure and causation.
1. Defendant is Liable for EMD Engines
Defendant contends that Plaintiff is suing EMD based on products that General Motors Corporation, or the former Electro-Motive Division of General Motors Corporation, manufactured and supplied.
2. Plaintiff's Exposure to Defendant's Products
Defendant argues that Plaintiff's deposition testimony does not establish exposure to products for which it is responsible. (Motion, 4.) The Court disagrees. Plaintiff testified that he "constantly" worked on EMD engines in the late 1970s and early 1980s aboard vessels, where working in engineering spaces and engine work was "a primary part" of his job description.
C. Motion for Summary Adjudication
Plaintiff does not oppose Defendant's motions for summary adjudication as to his Third Cause of Action for Negligent Misrepresentation and Fourth Cause of Action for Fraud by Nondisclosure, which are GRANTED.
Defendant's motion for summary adjudication as to the claim for punitive damages is DENIED.

DEFENDANT PERKINS ENGINES, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
(FOR DISPOSITION PURPOSES ONLY)
Off Calendar Mar-27-2025 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES: 3 of 3

C. Motion for Summary Adjudication
Plaintiff does not oppose Defendant's motions for summary adjudication of the Third Cause of Action for Negligent Misrepresentation and Fourth Cause of Action for Fraud by Nondisclosure, which are GRANTED.
Defendant's motion for summary adjudication as to the claim for punitive damages is DENIED. Defendant fails to meet its burden of demonstrating that Plaintiff will be unable to make a showing that Defendant acted with malice, fraud, or oppression. (Civ. Code 3294, subd. (a).) Plaintiff has provided evidence from the deposition testimony of Defendant's corporate representative, Mr. Klein, tending to show that Defendant was aware of asbestos hazards no later than 1972, but did not warn its users of the hazards of asbestos until 1986. (DMF No. 6, 18.) Punitive damages may be available when a defendant knows the dangers of asbestos, took action to protect its own employees, knew that its products were likely to pose a danger to users, and did not warn them. (Pfeifer v. John Crane, Inc. (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1270, 1300.) Such evidence is "sufficient to show malice, that is, despicable conduct coupled with conscious disregard for the safety of others." (Id. at pp. 1300-1301.) Given Defendant's knowledge of potential asbestos contamination of its products and its decision to not warn its users as to the risk of asbestos exposure, a reasonable finder of fact could infer that Defendant consciously disregarded the risk of asbestos contamination of its products. (See Barenborg v. Sigma Alpha Epsilon Fraternity (2019) 33 Cal.App.5th 70, 76 ["We view the evidence in the light most favorable to the nonmoving party and draw all reasonable inferences in its favor."].) Accordingly, the motion for summary adjudication as to Plaintiff's claim for punitive damages is denied. = (eps/304)

DEFENDANT PERKINS ENGINES, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
(FOR DISPOSITION PURPOSES ONLY)
Off Calendar Mar-27-2025 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES: 2 of 3

A plaintiff's ignorance is by itself insufficient to demonstrate that plaintiff lacks the ability to produce evidence supporting his claim and does not serve to shift defendant's initial burden. (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439.)
2. Plaintiff Can Establish Exposure to Defendant's Products
Defendant also contends that Plaintiff cannot establish that Defendant supplied the products at issue and that Defendant was not the exclusive distributor of Defendant's engines and replacement parts. (Motion, 1, 7-10.) The Court disagrees. Defendant's own admissions undermine its argument that Plaintiff cannot establish exposure. As Plaintiff notes, Defendant admitted in sworn responses to interrogatories that it distributed Perkins engines with asbestos-containing component parts since 1960. (DMF No. 6; Klein Decl. 4 ["Some of these engines may have contained encapsulated and/or bonded chrysotile asbestos during certain periods of time."].) Defendant's person most qualified also testified that that it was "typical of the industry" to have asbestos containing components in engines through at least the 1970's. (Id.) Plaintiff thus has presented evidence that Defendant's engines contained asbestos during the period in which he worked.
Defendant's reliance on the declaration of Defendant's corporate representative, Kevin Klein, to support its argument that Plaintiff cannot establish exposure to Defendant's products is unpersuasive. (Undisputed Material Facts "UMF" Nos. 21-34.) As mentioned above, as to periods preceding his employment by Defendant, Mr. Klein's declaration lacks foundation and constitutes inadmissible hearsay. (Evid. Code 702(a).) Plaintiff's testimony establishes that he used OEM gaskets on Perkins engines whenever possible, and he knew they were OEM products because "[t]hey came in the manufacturer's bag." (DMF No. 5.) While Mr. Klein asserts that Defendant was not the sole importer or distributer of Defendant's engines, his declaration is based "upon information and belief," and does not supply admissible evidence that the particular engines Plaintiff worked on were supplied by a different entity than Defendant. (Klein Decl. 7.)Even if Mr. Klein's declaration were admissible, Plaintiff has submitted the declaration of Dr. Michael Ellenbecker, which raises triable issues of fact. Dr. Ellenbecker, an industrial hygienist, in his expert opinion and based on reviewing the relevant documentation, opines that considering that Plaintiff's work with Defendant's engines was aboard marine vessels in the 1970s and 1980s, it is more likely than not that Defendant's engines contained asbestos components, including gaskets. (DMF Nos. 9-10.) Dr. Ellenbecker further opines that the exposures Plaintiff suffered from this work were significant and increased his risk of developing mesothelioma. (DMF No. 11.) As Plaintiff notes in opposition, to satisfy the first element of threshold exposure to Defendant's products under Lineaweaver v. Plant Insulation Co. (1995) 31 Cal.App.4th 1409, Plaintiff need only demonstrate some exposure "of whatever duration" so that the exposure "is a possible factor in causing the disease[.]" (Id. at 1415-16.) Plaintiff has made this showing. Here, Plaintiff's expert testimony, along with Defendant's own admission, is sufficient to establish a triable issue of material fact as to exposure and causation. Given this evidence, a reasonable juror could conclude that Defendant was responsible for Plaintiff's asbestos exposure. Thus, Defendant's motion for summary judgment is denied.

I. DEFENDANT PERKINS ENGINES, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
Defendant Perkins Engines, Inc.'s Motion for Summary Judgment or, in the Alternative, Motion for Summary Adjudication is DENIED. Opposition filed. Reply filed.
A. Evidentiary Objections
a. Plaintiff's Objections
" Declaration of Kevin Klein at 3-9, are SUSTAINED on grounds of lack of foundation and hearsay as to events and conditions prior to 1990. (LAOSD Asbestos Cases (2023) 87 Cal.App.5th 939, 947-951 [corporate representative who did not work at defendant until 1994 lacked personal knowledge regarding events prior to her employment]; Ramirez v. Avon Products (2023) 87 Cal.App.5th 939, 951 [a declaration by a corporate witness about activities before she started working at the corporation cannot be based on her personal knowledge and must be hearsay.]) In Mr. Klein's declaration, none of the facts he attests to is within the time period of his personal knowledge.
b. Defendant's Objections
" Exhibit A to the Declaration of Rabiah N. Oral, consisting of deposition testimony of Plaintiff Shultz, is OVERRULED.
" Exhibit B, Defendant's responses to Interrogatories are OVERRRULED.
" Exhibit C to the deposition testimony of Kevin Klein taken in the Payne matter is OVERRULED. The deposition testimony of Mr. Klein as Defendant's corporate representative comes within the exception to the hearsay rule for party admissions.
" Exhibit E to the Declaration of Michael Ellenbecker is OVERRULED. Mr. Ellenbecker's declaration shows he is an industrial hygienist and has access to documents and information regarding equipment manufactured and sold by Defendant. Mr. Ellenbecker's expertise qualifies him to testify as an expert. Questions about the depth or scope of his knowledge or expertise go to the weight, not the admissibility, of his testimony. (People v. Jackson (2016) 1 Cal.5th 269, 328, 205.)
B. Motion for Summary Judgment
Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden of showing Plaintiff Robert Shultz ("Plaintiff") does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain needed evidence of asbestos exposure and causation. (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855.) Defendant argues that Plaintiff lacks evidence to establish that he was exposed to asbestos from a product for which Defendant is liable. The Court disagrees. For the reasons set below, the Court finds that Defendant failed to sustain its initial burden and Plaintiff has provided sufficient evidence in opposition to establish triable issues of material fact with respect to both causation and punitive damages.

1. Plaintiff's Deposition Testimony
Defendant argues that Plaintiff's testimony precludes Defendant having been a source of his asbestos exposure. (Motion, 1, 4.) The Court disagrees. Plaintiff testified that that he worked on "numerous" Perkins engines, and his work on these engines dates from the mid-1970's through "this decade." (Plaintiff's Disputed Material Facts ("DMF") No. 3.) Moreover, Plaintiff testified that his work on the engines included "everything from preventative maintenance to major overhauls." (DMF No. 4.) Specifically, Plaintiff testified the work on Defendant's engines required the replacement of gaskets, which entailed removing the old gaskets by scraping them off and then using emery paper or wire to finish the job, which resulted in dust exposure. (DMF No. 5.) The fact that Plaintiff did not recall the first time he ever worked on Defendant's engines, or the specific details of his work, does not create an inference of non-exposure or of the inability to prove exposure though other sources of evidence than Plaintiff's testimony.
